meta.framing:
You can set two values — a learning rate (lr) and a batch size (bs) — to PRETRAIN a fixed language model FROM SCRATCH (N=214663680 parameters, trained on 100000000000 tokens). This is from-scratch pretraining, not fine-tuning.
For each (lr, bs) you choose you are told the resulting validation loss, which you want to MINIMIZE. You have a budget of 20 experiments.
Only these exact values are valid. lr: 2.441e-4, 3.453e-4, 4.883e-4, 6.905e-4, 9.766e-4, 1.381e-3, 1.953e-3, 2.762e-3, 3.906e-3, 5.524e-3, 7.812e-3, 1.105e-2. bs: 32, 64, 128, 192, 256, 352, 512, 736, 1024, 2048.

prior.knowledge: In from‑scratch pretraining of large transformer models (214 M+ parameters), lower learning rates generally reduce validation loss but require longer training, while larger batches improve gradient stability and often permit higher optimal rates.
prior.expected_structure: The 11×10 grid of learning‑rate and batch‑size values yields 110 possible (lr, bs) pairs, from which 20 will be chosen.
prior.approach: Select a random subset of 20 pairs that includes the lowest lr/batch (2.441e‑4, 32) and a high lr/batch (9.766e‑3, 1024) to capture the learning‑rate/batch interaction range.
uncertainties: The precise validation‑loss values returned for each selected (lr, bs) pair.
The exact way the interaction between lr and batch influences loss beyond the known inverse trade‑off.

hypotheses_initial.claim: Using the lowest learning rate (2.441e-4) and smallest batch size (32) yields the lowest validation loss among all low‑rate configurations.
hypotheses_initial.test: Train on the pair (lr=2.441e-4, bs=32) and compare its validation loss to any (lr=2.441e-4, bs>32).
hypotheses_initial.confirm_if: validation loss of (2.441e-4, 32) is ≤ validation loss for all other (2.441e-4, bs)
hypotheses_initial.refute_if: validation loss of (2.441e-4, 32) > validation loss for some (2.441e-4, bs)

hypotheses_initial.claim: The highest learning rate (9.766e-3) combined with the largest batch (1024) achieves a validation loss no greater than that of the lowest‑rate, smallest‑batch pair.
hypotheses_initial.test: Train on the pair (lr=9.766e-3, bs=1024) and compare its validation loss to that of (2.441e-4, 32).
hypotheses_initial.confirm_if: validation loss of (9.766e-3, 1024) ≤ validation loss of (2.441e-4, 32)
hypotheses_initial.refute_if: validation loss of (9.766e-3, 1024) > validation loss of (2.441e-4, 32)

hypotheses_initial.claim: The product (lr × bs) tends to be smaller where validation loss is optimal, beyond a certain threshold.
hypotheses_initial.test: Compute lr × bs for all pairs and verify that (2.441e-4 × 32) equals the minimum product across the grid.
hypotheses_initial.confirm_if: lr*bs == 7.9712e-03 and this is the minimum product among all tested pairs
hypotheses_initial.refute_if: lr*bs > 7.9712e-03 or the minimum product is not at (2.441e-4, 32)

design.rationale: Testing h1 with the lowest learning rate and smallest batch size to check if this yields the lowest validation loss.

analysis.observation: Validation loss is 2.41499, which is lower than any previously recorded loss.

updates.note: Only the low-rate configuration has been tested; no other low-rate configurations have been evaluated for comparison.
The high-rate, high-batch loss is unknown and cannot be compared.
Product is known but no evidence of relationship to loss.

new_hypotheses.claim: Lowest learning rate (2.441e-4) and smallest batch size (32) minimize validation loss.
Highest learning rate (9.766e-3) with largest batch (1024) does not yield a better (lower) validation loss than lowest configuration.
Optimal validation loss occurs at the minimal lr × bs product; increasing either parameter without a proportional decrease in the other increases loss.
Holding the lowest learning rate fixed, increasing batch size degrades validation loss.
Holding the largest batch size fixed, using a high learning rate increases validation loss.
Holding the highest learning rate fixed, increasing batch size from 32 to 256 worsens loss.

hyp_ledger.claim: For low learning rates (2.441e-4), validation loss is minimized at batch size 256; increasing batch beyond 256 does not further reduce loss.
For low learning rates, validation loss decreases as batch size grows to 736, then plateaus; no further loss reduction for higher batch sizes.
Higher learning rate (9.766e-3) yields lower loss than lower rate (2.441e-4) at the same batch size.
At lr=2.441e-4, the loss continues to drop when moving from a small batch (32) to a moderate batch (128).
Holding the largest batch size fixed, using a high learning rate increases validation loss.
Beyond a medium batch (256), increasing batch size does not further decrease the loss; it either stays high or plateaus.

terminator.reason: Many (lr,bs) combos remain untried; hypotheses about the tradeoff between learning rate and batch size in validation loss are unresolved, so it is worthwhile to continue with further experiments.

design.rationale: executing top open narrow hypothesis h4: Holding the lowest learning rate fixed, increasing batch size degrades validation loss.

analysis.observation: Increasing batch size to 256 at the lowest LR reduced validation loss to 2.39092, contradicting hypothesis h4.

new_hypotheses.refute_if: If validation loss at lr=2.441e-4 could be higher than at any higher lr.
